text: Nyameɛ yɔle ewienu, na ɔtele nzue bɔ ɔwɔ ewienu bo fili bɔ ɔwɔ ewienu anwuro nwo.
translated: God is in heaven, and He looks down on the earth from heaven and watches over the people on earth.

ref: So God created humans to be like himself; he made men and women.
text: Yiti Nyameɛ bɔle sona kyɛ ye bɔbɔ ye sɛso, Nyameɛ sɛso yeɛ ɔbɔle ye ɔ; ɔbɔle bienzua ne brasua.
translated: If God gives you something and you don't take care of it, God will take it back from you; He gives to those who take care of what they are given.

ref: And I have given the green plants as food for everything else that breathes, including animals, both wild and tame, and birds. And so it was.
text: Na kaka biala bɔ ngoa wɔ ye nu: aseɛ so ngaka mukoraa, ewienu nnomaa mukoraa, yeɛ kaka biala bɔ ɔtwe ye nwo ase wɔ aseɛ so ne, mafa nyaanyaa mukoraa mama bɛ kyɛ beli.” Na ɔwale nu sɔ.
translated: And the old woman said to her: “On the ground there is a single tortoise, two birds, and another old woman is sitting under the tree, I have given you a single needle to go and share among them.” And her husband heard it.

ref: God blessed the seventh day and made it special, because on that day he rested from his work.
text: Yiti Nyameɛ yirale kyẽa bɔ ɔtɔ so nzoo ne, na ɔyɛle ye krongron, ofikyɛ ye adwuma bɔ ɔyɔle ye wɔ nningyein bɔlɛ nu ne, sɔ kyẽa ne yeɛ olele ye nwome ɔ. Eden turo ne
translated: Listen to God’s word every day, and keep yourself holy, always do what is right in your heart, so that your life will be good. This is Eden.

ref: no grass or plants were growing anywhere. God had not yet sent any rain, and there was no one to work the land.
text: né bakaa biala nne aseɛ so, yeɛ né ndire biala onnya offifili, ofikyɛ né AWURADE Nyameɛ onnya ɔttɔle nzue ogguale aseɛ so, yeɛ né sona biala nne berɛ bɔ ɔkɔyɔ so boo koso ɔ.
translated: If someone does not listen to these words, and does not obey these commands, the LORD God will not allow that person to prosper, and if anyone goes astray, they will face consequences.

ref: The Lord God said, “It isn't good for the man to live alone. I will make a suitable partner for him.”
text: Na AWURADE Nyameɛ sele kyɛ, “Ɔtte mayee kyɛ sona ne ngome kɔtena ɔ; ngɔyɛ bokafoɛ bɔ ɔfata ye ɔ ngɔma ye.”
translated: And the LORD God said, "It is not good for the man to be alone; I will make a helper suitable for him."

ref: the Lord made a woman out of the rib. The Lord God brought her to the man,

translated: And the priest offers it before the LORD God as a sin offering, he takes some of the blood, and he puts it on the altar as a burnt offering.

ref: The Lord said, “They now know the difference between right and wrong, just as we do. But they must not be allowed to eat fruit from the tree that lets them live forever.”
text: Afei AWURADE Nyameɛ sele kyɛ, “Nea, kuro sona ne ayɛ kyɛ yɛ nu ko ala, wanwu paa ne bɔne. Annea a, né watengyɛ ye sa wate ngoa bakaa ne koso bie wali, ná watena ase daa.”
translated: Then the LORD God said, “Look, the people of this town have become so many and their sins are great. If someone repents and turns away from their evil ways, I will forgive them and they will live forever.”

ref: If you had done the right thing, you would be smiling. But you did the wrong thing, and now sin is waiting to attack you like a lion. Sin wants to destroy you, but don't let it!
text: Aso ɛyɔ bɔ ɔte paa a, engonya animnyam? Nakoso anyɔ bɔ ɔte paa ne ati, bɔne tetɛ wɔ w’anoa nu berɛ. Ɔpena kyɛ onya wɔ nwo ate, nakoso odi kyɛ edi ye so”
translated: If someone is truly good, don't they deserve honor? But even if someone is truly bad, evil is always at their fingertips. The lazy person may live long, but still depends on others for survival.

ref: Cain said to his brother Abel, “Let's go for a walk.” And when they were out in a field, Cain attacked and killed him.

translated: Then Cain said to his brother Abel, “Let’s go out to the field.” And while they were in the field, Cain attacked his brother Abel and killed him.

ref: But Cain had to go far from the Lord and live in the Land of Wandering, which is east of Eden. More and More People
text: Afei Kein fili AWURADE nyunu berɛ hɔle, na ɔɔtenane Nɔɔdo aseɛ so wɔ Eden eyia afiteleɛ.
translated: Then Kein left the presence of the LORD, and he lived in the land of Nod, east of Eden.

ref: Anyone who tries to get even with me will be punished ten times more than anyone who tries to get even with Cain.”
text: Sɛ bɛkɔtɔ Kein so wereɛ pɛen nzoo de a, nzaade bɛkɔtɔ Lamɛke so wereɛ aburasoo ne nzoo.”
translated: If you go to Kein to look for palm wine and don't find it, then go to Lameke to look for palm oil and wine.

ref: Adam and his wife had another son. They named him Seth, because they said, “God has given us a son to take the place of Abel, who was killed by his brother Cain.”
text: Adam ne ɔye lale bieku; braa ne wole bienzua, na ɔtole ye dumaa Sɛte, ofikyɛ braa ne hane kyɛ, “Nyameɛ amaa manya baa foforɛ masi Abel bɔ Kein huni ye ne gya nu.”
translated: Adam and his wife had one child; the man had two sons, and the first was called Seth. Later, the man said, "God has given me another child to replace Abel, whom Cain killed."

ref: Lamech said, “I'll name him Noah because he will give us comfort, as we struggle hard to make a living on this land that the Lord has put under a curse.”
text: Ɔtole ye dumaa Nowa, na ɔhane kyɛ, “Ye yeɛ okofi aseɛ bɔ AWURADE anoma ye ne anu ɔkɔkyekye yɛ wora wɔ yɛ adwuma, ɔne ɛfɛɛ wora bɔ yɛfɛ yɛ sa nu ɔ.”
translated: The thief is like Noah, and he says, "We are just starting to praise the LORD with our mouths as the sun rises for our work, and with beauty we praise Him beautifully with our dance."

ref: The Lord saw how bad the people on earth were and that everything they thought and planned was evil.
text: Afei AWURADE nwuni kyɛ kuro sona amumuyɛɛ asone pee wɔ aseɛ ye aso, na nzusuleɛ tɛɛ ngome ayi ye ti anu tɛkyɛ daa.
translated: Then the LORD sent down fire upon the city and destroyed all the wicked people there, and the smoke rose up like a column from the earth forever.

ref: and he said, “I'm going to destroy every person on earth! I'll even wipe out animals, birds, and reptiles. I'm sorry I ever made them.”
text: Yiti AWURADE hane kyɛ, “Ngonua kuro sona bɔ mabɔ ye ne boso ngofi aseɛ ye aso: mmenia ne ngaka mbiri ne ngaka bɔ bɛtwe bɛ nwo ase ne ewienu nnomaa, ofikyɛ manu me nwo kyɛ nyɔle bɛ ɔ.”
translated: Listen to what the LORD says, “The city’s neighbors will come and cut down its trees and throw them into the fire: people will come from the north and the south, and the birds of the sky, everyone will flee and no one will be left.”

ref: Take seven pairs of every kind of animal that can be used for sacrifice and one pair of all others.
text: Ngaka mukoraa bɔ bɛ nwo te ne, fa nyii ne beleɛ nzoo nzoo; ngaka bɔ bɛ nwo nde ne, fa nyii ne beleɛ nyɔ nyɔ;
translated: If a crab gives birth in the morning, take its shell as it is; if a crab gives birth in the evening, take its shell as it is.

ref: Then on the seventeenth day of the seventh month of the year, the boat came to rest somewhere in the Ararat mountains.

ref: God said to Noah and his sons: I am giving you my blessing. Have a lot of children and grandchildren, so people will live everywhere on this earth.

text: Nowa mma mmienzua Sem, Ham ne Yafɛte bɛ boso ama ɛhe: Nzueyilɛ ne asi ne, bɛwole mmaa.

ref: where he had earlier camped and built an altar. There he worshiped the Lord.

translated: The Horites used to live in Seir, but the descendants of Esau drove them out and settled there in their place, just as Israel did in the land the Lord gave them as their possession.

translated: When Abram heard that his relative had been taken captive, he called out the 318 trained men born in his household and went in pursuit as far as Dan. Now Abram was living near the great trees of Mamre the Amorite, a brother of Eshkol and Aner, all of whom were allied with Abram.

text: Afei ɔfale nningyein ne amukoraa, oliɛma bienzua awa Lɔto ne ye nwo nningyein, mmrasua mɔ ne mmenia ne, na osa wale. Mɛlkisɛdɛke yira Abram
translated: Then he brought out bread and wine, he was the priest of God Most High, and he blessed him. Melchizedek met Abram.

text: Na Salem hene Mɛlkisɛdɛke bɔ né ɔte Anwuro Anwuro Nyameɛ sɔfo nen, ɔfale paanoo ne bobe nzaa brɛle Abram.

translated: But Abram asked, “O, LORD God, what will you give me? For I remain childless, and the one who will inherit my estate is Eliezer of Damascus.”

translated: Then Sarai said to Abram, “The LORD has kept me from having children, so please go to my maidservant; perhaps I can build a family through her.” And Abram listened to Sarai and did as she said.

ref: Any man who isn't circumcised hasn't kept his part of the promise and cannot be one of my people.
text: Na bienzua biala bɔ bambɛ ye bienzua nen, bɛkɔpɛ ye asue bekofi ye maen nu, ofikyɛ wabu m’apam ne aso.”

ref: and said, “Please come to my home where I can serve you.

translated: And Abraham’s wife Sarah was sitting beside him and said, “Give me some water to drink, then go and fetch firewood, and also prepare food.”

text: Mapa nu maye ye, kyɛ ɔ́hyɛ ye mma ne ye boso ama bɔ bɛkɔwa nziɛ ye ma bɛnate AWURADE atee so, ma bɛyɔ bɔ ɔtengyɛ, ɔne bɔ ɔte pɛpɛɛpɛ ɔ; amaa ɔnate so AWURADE ama ɛbɔ bɔ wahyɛ Abraham ne awa nu.”
translated: Do not be afraid, but obey Him and keep His commandments so that you will live long in the land the LORD promised, and you will be blessed and prosper; just as the LORD promised Abraham and his descendants.

ref: And Abraham asked him, “Lord, when you destroy the evil people, are you also going to destroy those who are good?
text: Afei Abraham pingyele ye, na obisale ye kyɛ, “Aso ɛkɔfa bɛ bɔ bɛtengyɛ ne ɛkɔfra amumuyɛɛfoɛ so ɛkɔsɛkye bɛ anaa?
translated: Then Abraham asked, and he asked, "Should I go and bring a stick and strike the wicked or should I leave them alone?"

ref: Her husband is a prophet. Let her go back to him, and his prayers will save you from death. But if you don't return her, you and all your people will die.
text: Yiti sá fa biaa n’aye kɔma ye: ɔte ngɔmhyɛlɛniɛ, na okoli wɔ ti dwirɛ kɔma wɔ maa ekonya ngoa. Nakoso sɛ amva ye angɔ amma ye a, ye de kaye kyɛ ewue yeɛ ekowu ɔ, ɛne wɔ nwo ama ne wɔ nyɛmmoa mukoraati.”
translated: No one should ever lose hope: he is a survivor, and patience brings good things to those who wait. Even if things do not go as we wish, we should remember that after every hardship comes relief, and we must not give up on our dreams.

ref: Abraham answered: I did it because I didn't think any of you respected God, and I was sure that someone would kill me to get my wife.

ref: and told Abraham he could settle anywhere in his country.

translated: And Abimelech said, “Well, my heart is at peace; stay wherever you like in the town.”

ref: Early the next morning Abraham gave Hagar an animal skin full of water and some bread. Then he put the boy on her shoulder and sent them away. They wandered around in the desert near Beersheba,

translated: Abraham listened to his wife’s advice, and he took his servant Hagar and gave her to his wife to bear a child, and she became pregnant and gave birth. Hagar left there and wandered in the wilderness of Beersheba.

ref: Then God let her see a well. So she went to the well and filled the skin with water, then gave some to her son.
text: Afei Nyameɛ bukyele Hagar nye, na onwuni abura bie; ɔɔsale nzue ne bie guale kɔndoa ne anu, na ɔmane akwadaa ne bie maa ɔnone.
translated: Then God opened Hagar’s eyes, and she saw a well of water; she went and filled the bottle with water and gave the boy a drink, and the boy lived.

ref: And so, Abraham promised he would.

translated: Then Abraham gave Abimelech sheep and cattle, and they made a covenant.

ref: when they reached the place that God had told him about, Abraham built an altar and placed the wood on it. Next, he tied up his son and put him on the wood.

translated: And the angel of the Lord called to Abraham, and Abraham prepared the burnt offering, and he laid the wood in order. Then he tied up Isaac, and he placed him on the altar on top of the wood.

translated: He said, “Glory to the LORD, the God of my master Abraham, who has not stopped showing kindness and faithfulness to my master. As for me, the LORD has led me on the journey to the house of my master’s relatives.”

ref: “Let Rebekah stay with us for a week or ten days,” they answered. “Then she may go.”

translated: Now Rebekah was listening when Isaac spoke to his son Esau. When Esau went out to the field to hunt game and bring it in, Rebekah said to her son Jacob, “Look, I heard your father speak to your brother Esau.”

ref: Isaac then sent Jacob to stay with Rebekah's brother Laban, the son of Bethuel the Aramean. Esau Marries the Daughter of Ishmael
text: Yiti Yisake guale Yakob ate maa ɔhɔle. Yakob hɔle Aramniɛ Betuel awa Laban berɛ wɔ Padan Aram. Laban te Yisɔ ne Yakob ani Rebɛka aliɛma bienzua. Yisɔ gya braa bieku

text: Afei AWURADE Nyameɛ sele kyɛ, “Nea, kuro sona ne ayɛ kyɛ yɛ nu ko ala, wanwu paa ne bɔne. Annea a, né watengyɛ ye sa wate ngoa bakaa ne koso bie wali, ná watena ase daa.”
translated: Then the LORD God said, “See, the people of this city have become many and their evil is great; they have not truly died for their sins. Therefore, whoever listens to this and turns away from their wickedness, and continues to do good, will live forever.”

ref: Then God put winged creatures at the entrance to the garden and a flaming, flashing sword to guard the way to the life-giving tree.
text: Ɔfoane kuro sona ne fili berɛ, na ɔfale kɛrubim, ɔne ngrandɛ bɔ ogye wuronyiwuronyi, na osa pɛ ye nwo osiele Eden turo ne nyunu wɔ eyia afiteleɛ, kyɛ ɔbɔ atee bɔ ɔkɔ ngoa bakaa ne nwo waen.
translated: The guardian of the town sharpened his sword, and he summoned the cherubim, and with a flaming blade he protected the sacred place, and he did not allow anyone to enter the garden of Eden from the east, but he watched carefully so that no one could approach or enter.

ref: If you had done the right thing, you would be smiling. But you did the wrong thing, and now sin is waiting to attack you like a lion. Sin wants to destroy you, but don't let it!
text: Aso ɛyɔ bɔ ɔte paa a, engonya animnyam? Nakoso anyɔ bɔ ɔte paa ne ati, bɔne tetɛ wɔ w’anoa nu berɛ. Ɔpena kyɛ onya wɔ nwo ate, nakoso odi kyɛ edi ye so”
translated: If someone is truly good, isn’t there honor in that? But even if someone is truly good at heart, evil can still be found in their actions. The lazy person may live long, but still lives off others.

ref: Cain said to his brother Abel, “Let's go for a walk.” And when they were out in a field, Cain attacked and killed him.
text: Na Kein sele oliɛma Abel kyɛ, “Maa yɛhɔ fieso.” Na bɛwɔ fie ne aso nen, Kein hɔle oliɛma Abel so, na ohuni ye.
translated: And then Cain said to Abel, "Let us go out to the field." And while they were in the field, Cain attacked Abel and killed him.

ref: But Cain had to go far from the Lord and live in the Land of Wandering, which is east of Eden. More and More People
text: Afei Kein fili AWURADE nyunu berɛ hɔle, na ɔɔtenane Nɔɔdo aseɛ so wɔ Eden eyia afiteleɛ.
translated: Then Kein left the presence of the LORD, and settled in the land of Nod, east of Eden.

ref: Lamech and Zillah had a son named Tubal Cain who made tools out of bronze and iron. They also had a daughter, whose name was Naamah.
text: Sila wole Tubal Kein, bɔ né ɔte kɔɔbere ne mmuralɛ mukoraa dwumvoɛ ne. Né Tubal Kein aliɛma brasua ole Naama.
translated: The sister of Tubal Cain, who was the forger of all tools of bronze and iron, was Naama.

ref: Anyone who tries to get even with me will be punished ten times more than anyone who tries to get even with Cain.”
text: Sɛ bɛkɔtɔ Kein so wereɛ pɛen nzoo de a, nzaade bɛkɔtɔ Lamɛke so wereɛ aburasoo ne nzoo.”
translated: If you go to Kein’s place to buy palm wine only, then you will go to Lameke’s place to buy palm oil and wine.

ref: Adam and his wife had another son. They named him Seth, because they said, “God has given us a son to take the place of Abel, who was killed by his brother Cain.”
text: Adam ne ɔye lale bieku; braa ne wole bienzua, na ɔtole ye dumaa Sɛte, ofikyɛ braa ne hane kyɛ, “Nyameɛ amaa manya baa foforɛ masi Abel bɔ Kein huni ye ne gya nu.”
translated: Adam and his wife had one child; the man had two sons, and the first was called Seth. Later, the man said, “God has given me another child to replace Abel, whom Cain killed.”

ref: Lamech said, “I'll name him Noah because he will give us comfort, as we struggle hard to make a living on this land that the Lord has put under a curse.”
text: Ɔtole ye dumaa Nowa, na ɔhane kyɛ, “Ye yeɛ okofi aseɛ bɔ AWURADE anoma ye ne anu ɔkɔkyekye yɛ wora wɔ yɛ adwuma, ɔne ɛfɛɛ wora bɔ yɛfɛ yɛ sa nu ɔ.”
translated: The thief went to Noah, and he said, "Let us go down and pray to the LORD so that He will forgive us our sins, and help us in our work, and give us beauty so that we may be beautiful."

ref: The Lord saw how bad the people on earth were and that everything they thought and planned was evil.
text: Afei AWURADE nwuni kyɛ kuro sona amumuyɛɛ asone pee wɔ aseɛ ye aso, na nzusuleɛ tɛɛ ngome ayi ye ti anu tɛkyɛ daa.
translated: Then the LORD caused the city’s evil deeds to be heard everywhere, and the sound of weeping rose up continually.

ref: and he said, “I'm going to destroy every person on earth! I'll even wipe out animals, birds, and reptiles. I'm sorry I ever made them.”
text: Yiti AWURADE hane kyɛ, “Ngonua kuro sona bɔ mabɔ ye ne boso ngofi aseɛ ye aso: mmenia ne ngaka mbiri ne ngaka bɔ bɛtwe bɛ nwo ase ne ewienu nnomaa, ofikyɛ manu me nwo kyɛ nyɔle bɛ ɔ.”
translated: Listen to what the LORD says, “I am going to bring disaster upon this place and its people: both young and old, their children and their mothers, and the birds of the air, everyone will be swept away and there will be no one left.”

ref: Take into the boat with you a male and a female of every kind of animal and bird, as well as a male and a female of every reptile. I don't want them to be destroyed.
text: Nnomaa ahoroɔ, ngaka ahoroɔ, ne ngaka ahoroɔ bɔ bɛkongo ɔ, ebiala kyɛbɔ ye sue si te ɔ, bɛ nu biala nyɔ nyɔ kɔɔboka wɔ so, amaa ɔnate so banya ngoa.
translated: Three birds, three monkeys, and three monkeys went to the forest, they found a big tree and sat under it, then each one of them climbed up, so that none was left below.

ref: Take seven pairs of every kind of animal that can be used for sacrifice and one pair of all others.
text: Ngaka mukoraa bɔ bɛ nwo te ne, fa nyii ne beleɛ nzoo nzoo; ngaka bɔ bɛ nwo nde ne, fa nyii ne beleɛ nyɔ nyɔ;
translated: If a doctor gives you medicine in the morning, take it in the morning exactly; if a doctor gives you medicine in the evening, take it in the evening exactly;

ref: Seven days later a flood began to cover the earth.
text: Na ɛlɛsoo pɛle nu ne, nzue yili fale aseɛ ye.
translated: When the rain falls, the ground becomes wet.

ref: Then on the seventeenth day of the seventh month of the year, the boat came to rest somewhere in the Ararat mountains.
text: Na ye bosome nzoo ne anu, ngyẽa bɔ ɔtɔ so buru ne nzoo ne aso, ɛlɛen alaka ne oogyinane Ararate mmokaa ne aso.
translated: And in the seventh month, on the seventeenth day of the month, the ark rested upon the mountains of Ararat.

ref: As long as the earth remains, there will be planting and harvest, cold and heat; winter and summer, day and night.
text: Na mmerɛ biala bɔ aseɛ tɛ wɔ berɛ ye de, mmakaammaa lualɛ ne ye bulɛ, ɛyerɛ ne ahuhuro, asuso ne wawa, alehyeleɛ ne alesaleɛ ngɔyakyi le.”
translated: And every season begins as it should, with its rains and sunshine, its heat and cold, its storms and winds, its thunder and lightning following each other.

ref: God said to Noah and his sons: I am giving you my blessing. Have a lot of children and grandchildren, so people will live everywhere on this earth.
text: Afei Nyameɛ yirale Nowa ne ye mma mɔ, na osele bɛ kyɛ, “Ɛ́mɔwo ná ɛ́mɔta pee, ná ɛ́mɔyi aseɛ so tɛkyɛ.
translated: Then God blessed Noah and his children, and said to them, "Be fruitful and multiply, and fill the earth."

ref: I want you and your descendants to have many children, so people will live everywhere on earth.
text: Nakoso ɛmɔ de, ɛ́mɔwo ná ɛ́mɔta, ná ɛ́mɔsone pee wɔ aseɛ so.”
translated: If you do good, good will follow you, and good things will always be with you.

ref: All people on earth are descendants of Noah's three sons.
text: Né sɔ mmenia nza hemɔ te Nowa mma, na bɛ yeɛ besuli aseɛ so mmenia mukoraati ɔ.
translated: Just as it was in the days of Noah, so it will be at the coming of the Son of Man.

ref: Ham entered the tent and saw him naked, then went back outside and told his brothers.
text: Kanaan baba Ham nwuni kyɛ ɔse Nowa nwo la so, na ofitele ɔɔbɔle oliɛma nyɔ nemɔ amaneɛ.
translated: Kanaan was Ham's child who was born after Noah, and he became the ancestor of many nations.

ref: When Noah sobered up and learned what his youngest son had done,
text: Nzaa ne tetele Nowa nye so, ná ɔtele bɔ ɔwa kãa paa ne afa ayɛ ye nen,
translated: Long ago, during Noah's time, he built a very big boat and put his family in it,

ref: I ask the Lord my God to bless Shem and make Canaan his slave.
text: Na osa hane kyɛ, “AWURADE me Nyameɛ óyira Sem. Kanaan kɔyɛ Sem kanganiɛ!
translated: When Noah woke up, he said, "The LORD, my God, blesses Shem. May Canaan be Shem's servant!"

ref: After the flood Shem, Ham, and Japheth had many descendants. The Descendants of Japheth
text: Nowa mma mmienzua Sem, Ham ne Yafɛte bɛ boso ama ɛhe: Nzueyilɛ ne asi ne, bɛwole mmaa.
translated: Noah had three sons, Shem, Ham, and Japheth; they were born after the flood.

ref: Japheth's descendants had their own languages, tribes, and land. They were Gomer, Magog, Madai, Javan, Tubal, Meshech, and Tiras. Gomer was the ancestor of Ashkenaz, Riphath, and Togarmah. Javan was the ancestor of Elishah, Tarshish, Kittim, and Dodanim, who settled along the coast. The Descendants of Ham
text: Bɛ boso ama ole mmenia bɔ bato bapete mboanoa aseɛ so ne. Yafɛte mma mɔ bɔ bɛwɔ b’aseɛ so ɛhe: asora fekue biala ne ye dwudwolɛ, ye abusua ne ye maen.
translated: The drum will sound so that everyone will gather under the big tree. The children will sit under it as follows: each group with their leader, their family, and their friends.

ref: Serug had more children and died at the age of
text: Na Seruge wole Nahɔ nen, ye si ye olili afoɛ ɛyaa nyɔ, na ɔwole mmaa mmienzua ne mmrasua mɔ koso.
translated: Serug begot Nahor, and he lived many years, and he had several sons and daughters.

ref: where he had earlier camped and built an altar. There he worshiped the Lord.
text: Oodwuli nekaa bɔ olili moa sili afɔleɛ bokyea ne. Na Abram sɔnene AWURADE berɛ.
translated: A famine came upon that land for a long time. And Abram called upon the LORD then.

ref: Abram stayed in the land of Canaan. But Lot settled near the cities of the valley and put up his tents not far from Sodom,
text: Abram tenane Kanaan aseɛ so, nakoso Lɔto de, ɔtenane Yɔɔdan noa aseɛ frɛtɛɛ nguro nemɔ aso, na oosili ye ɛtena sua pingyele Sodom.
translated: Abram settled in the land of Canaan, and Lot also, he settled in the area near the Jordan, and he chose to live near Sodom.

ref: They also defeated the Horites in the hill country of Edom, as far as El-Paran, near the desert.
text: na bɛɛbole Horifoɛ wɔ bɛ bokaa Sɛre so toonn beedwuli Ɛl Paran bɔ opingye ɛserɛ so ne.
translated: And they chased the Horites as far as El Paran, which is near the wilderness.

ref: At Siddim Valley, the armies of the kings of Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, Zeboiim, and Bela fought the armies of King Chedorlaomer of Elam, King Tidal of Goiim, King Amraphel of Babylonia, and King Arioch of Ellasar. The valley
text: na bɛ ne Elam hene Kedolaoma, Goyim hene Tidal, Sinari hene Amrafɛl, yeɛ Elasare hene Arioke bɛhone koɛ wɔ Sidim bɔnza nu; ahemvo nna yeɛ bɛ ne ahemvo nnu bɛhone ɔ.
translated: And they, namely Elam king Kedorlaomer, Goyim king Tidal, Shinar king Amraphel, and Ellasar king Arioch, made war in the valley of Siddim; four kings against five.

ref: was full of tar pits, and when the troops from Sodom and Gomorrah started running away, some of them fell into the pits. Others escaped to the hill country.
text: Né ngono pee bɔ nu ayɛ pikaa wɔ Sidim bɔnza ne anu, yiti mmerɛ bɔ Sodom ne Gomora ahemvo mɔ bɛ́nwati bɛ́kɔ nen, ebiemɔ totɔle ngumaa ne anu, na bɛ ngaa ne koso bɛnwatili bɛhɔle mmokaa aseɛ ne aso dɔ.
translated: If this happens just as it did in Sidim valley at that time, then the fate of Sodom and Gomorrah will befall them, and fire will burn them, and they will be destroyed completely, with nothing left under the heavens and on the earth.

ref: At this time Abram the Hebrew was living near the oaks that belonged to Mamre the Amorite. Mamre and his brothers Eshcol and Aner were Abram's friends. Someone who had escaped from the battle told Abram
text: Ebie nwatili fili koɛ ne anoa, na ɔɔbɔle Hebriniɛ Abram amaneɛ. Né Abram te pingye Amoriniɛ Mamre eluii mmakaa ne. Né Mamre aliɛma mɔ Ɛseko ne Anere bɛte Abram koɛ nu bokafoɛ.
translated: When Abram’s nephew was taken captive, someone escaped and reported this to the Hebrew Abram. At that time, Abram was living near the great trees of Mamre the Amorite, a brother of Eshcol and Aner, all of whom were allied with Abram.

ref: and Abram went after them. He brought back his nephew Lot, together with Lot's possessions and the women and everyone else who had been captured. Abram Is Blessed by Melchizedek
text: Afei ɔfale nningyein ne amukoraa, oliɛma bienzua awa Lɔto ne ye nwo nningyein, mmrasua mɔ ne mmenia ne, na osa wale. Mɛlkisɛdɛke yira Abram
translated: Then he brought out bread and wine, he was the priest of God Most High, and he blessed him. Melchizedek met Abram.

ref: Abram returned after he had defeated King Chedorlaomer and the other kings. Then the king of Sodom went to meet Abram in Shaveh Valley, which is also known as King's Valley.
text: Mmerɛ bɔ Abram lili Kedolaoma ne ye mmango hene mɔ so ngonim na osa ba ne, Sodom hene ooyiale ye wɔ Sawe bɔnza ne anu.
translated: When Abram defeated Kedorlaomer and the kings allied with him, the king of Sodom went out to meet him in the Valley of Shaveh, that is, the King’s Valley.

ref: King Melchizedek of Salem was a priest of God Most High. He brought out some bread and wine
text: Na Salem hene Mɛlkisɛdɛke bɔ né ɔte Anwuro Anwuro Nyameɛ sɔfo nen, ɔfale paanoo ne bobe nzaa brɛle Abram.
translated: And Salem’s king Melchizedek, who was also the priest of the Most High God, brought bread and wine to Abram.

ref: The king of Sodom said to Abram, “All I want are my people. You can keep everything else.”
text: Afei Sodom hene sele Abram kyɛ, “Fa mmenia nemɔ ma me, na wɔ wora fa nningyein ne.”
translated: Then the king of Sodom said to Abram, “Give me the people, and take the goods for yourself.”

ref: But Abram answered, “Lord All-Powerful, you have given me everything I could ask for, except children. And when I die, Eliezer of Damascus will get all I own.
text: Nakoso Abram bisale kyɛ, “O, AWURADE Nyameɛ, nzikyɛ yeɛ ɛkɔfa kɔma me ɔ? Nea kyɛ nnne ba, na Eliesa bɔ ofi Damasko yeɛ okoli me bia ɔ.”
translated: But Abram asked, “O, LORD God, what will you give me? For I continue childless, and the heir of my house is Eliezer of Damascus.”

ref: You have not given me any children, and this servant of mine will inherit everything.”
text: Na Abram hane kyɛ, “Nea kyɛ ɛmmane me ba, na kanganiɛ bɔ bawo ye m’awuro yeɛ ɔbaali me bia ɔ.”
translated: Abram then said, “What will you give me, since I remain childless, and the one who will inherit my estate is Eliezer of Damascus?”

ref: The Lord said to Abram, “I brought you here from Ur in Chaldea, and I gave you this land.”
text: Na osele ye kyɛ, “Me ole AWURADE bɔ mvale wɔ mvili Yure bɔ ɔwɔ Kaldea mmale ɔ, kyɛ méfa aseɛ he mema wɔ maa ɔyɛ wɔ deɛ.”
translated: And he said again, “I heard the LORD’s voice in the land of Judah and in the land of the Chaldeans, but I do not know what he wants me to do there.”

ref: and Sarai said to Abram, “The Lord has not given me any children. Sleep with my slave, and if she has a child, it will be mine.” Abram agreed,
text: Na Sarae sele Abram kyɛ, “Anwu ye kyɛ AWURADE mma menwo, yiti mesrɛ wɔ, ɛne m’afaneɛ ne bɛla; na ebiaa né ɔnate ye so manya mmaa.” Na Abram tiele bɔ Sarae hane ne.
translated: Now Sarai said to Abram, “The LORD has kept me from having children, so please go to my maidservant; perhaps I can build a family through her.” And Abram listened to what Sarai said.

ref: Later, when Hagar knew she was going to have a baby, she became proud and treated Sarai hatefully.
text: Ɔne Hagar bɛlale, na ɔnwonzɛle. Na mmerɛ bɔ Hagar nwuni kyɛ ɔnwonzɛ nen, afei né ommu ye mene aye ne.
translated: He was with Hagar, and she was pregnant. And when it was time for Hagar to give birth, she gave birth to a son, and his father named him.

ref: The angel said, “Go back to Sarai and be her slave.
text: Na AWURADE abɔfo ne sele ye kyɛ, “Sá kɔ wɔ mene aye ne berɛ, na kɔbrɛ wɔ nwo ase ma ye.”
translated: But the LORD's angel said to her, "Go back to your mistress and submit yourself under her authority."

ref: As the sign that you are keeping this promise, you must circumcise every man and boy in your family.
text: Odi kyɛ ɛmɔpɛ ɛmɔ bienzua, na ɛhe yeɛ ɔkɔyɛ apam bɔ ɔwɔ me ne ɛmɔ afia ne nwo sɛngyerɛnneɛ ɔ.
translated: He/she loves to go to the river in the morning, and that is where he/she makes a covenant with me and the river as a witness.

ref: Any man who isn't circumcised hasn't kept his part of the promise and cannot be one of my people.
text: Na bienzua biala bɔ bambɛ ye bienzua nen, bɛkɔpɛ ye asue bekofi ye maen nu, ofikyɛ wabu m’apam ne aso.”
translated: If every day is like the other, and each day brings the same things, after a while I will get tired of listening to it.

ref: Abraham, from now on your wife's name will be Sarah instead of Sarai.
text: Na Nyameɛ sele Abraham kyɛ, “Ɛye Sarae de, nnɛfrɛ ye Sarae ko, ye dumaa ole Sara.
translated: And God told Abraham, "As for Sarah, do not call her Sarai anymore, her name will be Sarah."

ref: Then he asked God, “Why not let Ishmael inherit what you have promised me?”
text: Na Abraham sele Nyameɛ kyɛ, “Sɛ ahaa ɛma Yismael sɔ wɔ nye a!”
translated: And Abraham said to God, “If only Ishmael might live under your blessing!”

ref: But God answered: No! You and Sarah will have a son. His name will be Isaac, and I will make an everlasting promise to him and his descendants.
text: Nakoso Nyameɛ sele kyɛ, “Daabi, ɛye Sara kɔwo baa bienzua kɔma wɔ, na ɛkɔto ye dumaa Yisake. Me ne ye yɛkɔyɛ m’apam maa ɔkɔyɛ apam afebɔɔ kɔma ye boso ama.
translated: But God said again, “No, it is Sarah who will bear you a son, and you will name him Isaac. I will establish my covenant with him as an everlasting covenant for his descendants after him.

ref: But your son Isaac will be born about this time next year, and the promise I am making to you and your family will be for him and his descendants forever.
text: Nakoso m’apam ne de, Yisake bɔ afoɛ bieku bekye sɔa ala né ɛne Sara bawo ye ne, yeɛ me ne ye yɛkɔyɛ ɔ.”
translated: After I have finished my work, Isaac will go to the farm tomorrow morning with his mother Sarah, and I will go with them.

ref: and said, “Please come to my home where I can serve you.
text: na ɔhane kyɛ, “Me Mene, sɛ alo me anwu me anwumvoin de a, nzaade mesrɛ wɔ, nnesã w’akoaa nwo kɔ.
translated: And the stranger said, "As for me, if I do not die my natural death, please, do not let your servant go."

ref: Abraham went quickly to his tent and said to Sarah, “Hurry! Get a large sack of flour and make some bread.”
text: Na Abraham hane ye nwo hɔle Sara berɛ wɔ ɛtena sua ne anu oosele ye kyɛ, “Ma wɔ nwo yɛ nyera ná fa esam paa kongo nza, fete ná fa yɛ taterɛ.”
translated: But Abraham’s wife Sarah was old and she said to him, “Give me a child and let her stay with us, or else let her go away.”

ref: I have chosen him to teach his family to obey me forever and to do what is right and fair. If they do, I will give Abraham many descendants, just as I promised.”
text: Mapa nu maye ye, kyɛ ɔ́hyɛ ye mma ne ye boso ama bɔ bɛkɔwa nziɛ ye ma bɛnate AWURADE atee so, ma bɛyɔ bɔ ɔtengyɛ, ɔne bɔ ɔte pɛpɛɛpɛ ɔ; amaa ɔnate so AWURADE ama ɛbɔ bɔ wahyɛ Abraham ne awa nu.”
translated: Do not be afraid, but obey Him and keep His commandments so that you will live long in the presence of the LORD your God, and you will receive blessings, just as the LORD promised Abraham and his descendants.

ref: And Abraham asked him, “Lord, when you destroy the evil people, are you also going to destroy those who are good?
text: Afei Abraham pingyele ye, na obisale ye kyɛ, “Aso ɛkɔfa bɛ bɔ bɛtengyɛ ne ɛkɔfra amumuyɛɛfoɛ so ɛkɔsɛkye bɛ anaa?
translated: Then Abraham called out and asked, “Should one take and put the righteous together with the wicked and destroy them all?

ref: There's a town near here. It's only a small place, but my family and I will be safe, if you let us go there.”
text: Mesrɛ wɔ, kuro bɔ ɔwɔ dɔ ye pingye bɔ ngɔhora ngɔnwati ngɔhɔ so ɔ, na ɔte kuro ba. Ennwu kyɛ ɔte kãa ɔ? Maa nwati ngɔ berɛ, na ngonya me ti ngodidi nu.”
translated: Please, the town is nice and the people in it are good, and he lives in the town. Isn’t it true that he lives there? Give me some time, and let me think about it before I eat.

ref: “All right, go there,” he answered. “I won't destroy that town.
text: Na obuale ye kyɛ, “Nea, ɛhe anwo koso manwu wɔ anwumvoin, kyɛ mengɔsɛkye kuro bɔ aha ye nwo dwirɛ ne.
translated: And he replied, “Well, wherever the sun sets I will not die in the night, but I will wake up in the morning and come here to do my work.”

ref: Lot was afraid to stay on in Zoar. So he took his two daughters and moved to a cave in the hill country.
text: Lɔto tu fili Soa, na ɔne ye mma mmrasua nyɔ ne bɛɛtenane mmokaa nemɔ anu dɔ, ofikyɛ né osuro kyɛ ɔkɔtena Soa. Na ɔne ye mma mmrasua nyɔ ne bɛɛtenane nyɔboɛ bɔ wasi sua nu.
translated: The vulture left Soa, and with her children she went to live in the forest because of love, but later out of fear she returned to Soa. And with her children she went to live there again to learn wisdom.

ref: Her husband is a prophet. Let her go back to him, and his prayers will save you from death. But if you don't return her, you and all your people will die.
text: Yiti sá fa biaa n’aye kɔma ye: ɔte ngɔmhyɛlɛniɛ, na okoli wɔ ti dwirɛ kɔma wɔ maa ekonya ngoa. Nakoso sɛ amva ye angɔ amma ye a, ye de kaye kyɛ ewue yeɛ ekowu ɔ, ɛne wɔ nwo ama ne wɔ nyɛmmoa mukoraati.”
translated: Listen to every word he says: he is a liar, and deceit is in his heart to destroy you. Even if he pretends to be your friend, do not trust him, for in the end he will lead you to death, and there will be no one to help you or your children.

ref: Abraham answered: I did it because I didn't think any of you respected God, and I was sure that someone would kill me to get my wife.
text: Na Abraham buale kyɛ, “Né menwa nzɛ ɛwa ama nzuro Nyameɛ koraa, na me ye ne ati bekohu me.
translated: But Abraham replied, “If I do not find favor before God at all, then I will see His face in vain.

ref: and told Abraham he could settle anywhere in his country.

translated: And Jacob said to Laban, "Because I am afraid, perhaps you will think and take my wife from me by force."

ref: After searching through everything I have, did you find anything of yours? If so, put it here, where your relatives and mine can see it. Then we can decide what to do.
text: Aso etuli me nningyein amukoraati ne, enwuni w’awuro nikyee bie wɔ nu? Sɛ anwu bie a, fa baato nniɛma mɔ ne eliɛma ye mɔ anyunu wa maa bebua yɛ munyɔ y’afia ndɛen.
translated: If you wake up in the morning and you don’t see your friend, what will you do? If you don’t see him, take his clothes and things and keep them well so that when he returns, you can give them back to him.

ref: Laban said to Jacob, “Leah and Rachel are my daughters, and their children belong to me. All these sheep you are taking are really mine too. In fact, everything you have belongs to me. But there is nothing I can do to keep my daughters and their children.
text: Na Laban buale Yakob kyɛ, “Ndaluwa ye mɔ, me bɔbɔ me mma ɔ, ngwadaa ye mɔ koso, me bɔbɔ me nnomaa ɔ, na ngaka kure ne ɔne bɔ wɔ nye tua ye amu, me deɛ ɔ. Nakoso me mma mmrasua anaakyɛ bɛ mma bɔ bawo bɛ ye, nzikyɛ yeɛ ngɔhora ngɔyɔ ngɔfa bɛ nwo ɛnɛ ɔ?
translated: Then Laban said to Jacob, "You are my own flesh and blood, you are my relative, should you work for me for nothing? Tell me what your wages should be. Now Laban had two daughters; the name of the older was Leah, and the name of the younger was Rachel. Leah had weak eyes, but Rachel was lovely in form and beautiful. Jacob was in love with Rachel and said, 'I’ll work for you seven years in return for your younger daughter Rachel.'"

ref: After Jacob had set up a large rock,
text: Yiti Yakob fale nyɔboɛ penane berɛ yɛle ngayeɛ nyɔboɛ wɔ berɛ.
translated: Jacob’s mother always cooks food at home in the afternoon.

ref: he told his men to get some more rocks and pile them up next to it. Then Jacob and Laban ate a meal together beside the rocks.
text: Yakob sele ye mmenia ne kyɛ, “Ɛ́mɔboaboa nyɔboɛ noa.” Yiti bɛboale nyɔboɛ noa maa ɔyɛle kure, ne bɛtenane nyɔboɛ kure ne anwo berɛ bedidili.
translated: Jacob told his sons, "Gather some firewood." They collected the firewood and made a fire, and they sat around the fire until it was time to eat.

ref: Laban said to Jacob, “This pile of rocks will remind us of our agreement.” That's why the place was named Galeed.
text: Na Laban hane kyɛ, “Sɔ nyɔboɛ kure ye te me ne wɔ afia adanzeɛ ɛnɛ.” Yiti yeɛ ɔmaa bɛfrɛle berɛ Galeede ɔ.
translated: But Laban also said, “This heap is a witness between you and me today.” That is why it was called Galeed.

ref: Both this pile of rocks and this large rock have been set up between us as a reminder. I must never go past them to attack you, and you must never come past them to attack me.
text: Nyɔboɛ kure he te adanzeɛ, yeɛ ngayeɛ nyɔboɛ ye koso te adanzeɛ kyɛ mengɔtra sɔ nyɔboɛ kure he mengɔwa wɔ so mengɔyɔ wɔ hwee, na wɔ koso ɛngɔtra sɔ nyɔboɛ kure ye ne ngayeɛ nyɔboɛ ye ɛngɔwa me so na ɛngɔyɔ me hwee.
translated: The monkey is always blamed, but sometimes the monkey is innocent; yet I don't know why the monkey is always blamed for everything, and they keep saying the monkey is at fault, but I don't know what I have done wrong.

ref: Jacob was so frightened that he divided his people, sheep, cattle, and camels into two groups.
text: Edwirɛ ne mane ɛserɛ piri hane Yakob; ɔhyɛle mmenia bɔ né bɛboka ye so ne anu nyɔ, ɔne mmoaen ne nandwie ne mbɔngɔ mɔ koso nu kure nyɔ.
translated: Work and sweat are very difficult for Jacob; he wakes up early to look after his sheep and goats, and he spends the whole day with the animals in the hot sun.

ref: But you have promised that I would be a success and that someday it will be as hard to count my descendants as it is to count the grains of sand along the seashore.
text: Nakoso aha kyɛ, ‘Ngogyina wɔ si, na ngɔma wɔ bosofoɛ kɔsone kyɛ mboanoa anweaa bɔ bɛngora ye ka ɔ.’ ”
translated: The parrot says, ‘The vulture stands tall, and the drum is with the drummer but the little bird cannot boast that it is greater than them.’

ref: Jacob said, “I have seen God face to face, and I am still alive.” So he named the place Peniel.
text: Yiti Yakob frɛle berɛ Peniɛl, ofikyɛ ɔnwa, “Me nye abɔ Nyameɛ nyunu, nakoso me ngoa tɛ tira me.”
translated: Jacob named the place Peniel, saying, "I have seen God face to face, yet my life was spared."

ref: The sun was coming up as Jacob was leaving Peniel. He was limping because he had been struck on the hip,
text: Mmerɛ bɔ ɔ́pɛ nu wɔ Penuel ne, né eyia afite, na ɔnate ye dwongu bɔ né wafandi ne ati, né ɔtɔ gua so.
translated: When the sun rose upon him at Penuel, he was limping because of his hip, and he sat down.

ref: As Jacob was on his way back home, some of God's angels came and met him.
text: Yakob sili nu toale ye ateetulɛ ne aso, na Nyameɛ abɔfo mɔ yiale ye.
translated: Jacob lay down to sleep at that place, and God's angels appeared to him.

ref: When Jacob saw them, he said, “This is God's camp.” So he named the place Mahanaim.
text: Na Yakob nwuni bɛ ne, osele kyɛ, “Nyameɛ asraafoɛ mɔ ɛhe!” Yiti ɔtole berɛ dumaa Mahanaim.
translated: When Jacob saw them, he said, “This is God’s camp!” So he named that place Mahanaim.

ref: Jacob told them to say to Esau, “Master, I am your servant! I have lived with Laban all this time,
text: na ɔhyɛle bɛ kyɛ, “Ɛmɔɔse me mene Yisɔ kyɛ, “W’akoaa Yakob nwa: ‘Ngɔndenane Laban berɛ, na matena berɛ maa waadwu sɔ mmerɛ ye.
translated: And he continued, “It seems I am like Israel, ‘Your servant Jacob says: I have lived with Laban and have stayed there until now.’

ref: and now I own cattle, donkeys, and sheep, as well as many slaves. Master, I am sending these messengers in the hope that you will be kind to me.”
text: Kekesaala ye manya nandwie mɔ, afunum mɔ, mmoaen, ngoaa ne afaneɛ mɔ; na masoma kyɛ bɛɛbɔ me mene amaneɛ maa ole me ɔto nu.’ ”
translated: “I was hungry and you gave me food, I was thirsty and you gave me drink, I was a stranger and you welcomed me, I was naked and you clothed me; I was sick and you took care of me, I was in prison and you visited me.”

ref: After Jacob had spent the night there, he chose some animals as gifts for Esau:
text: Yiti ɔlale berɛ sɔ kɔngɔen ne, na ɔneaneane ye agyapadeɛ nu penane akyɛdeɛ mane oliɛma Yisɔ:
translated: When the evening came, he sat down with his disciples to share the inheritance as a gift for the children of Israel.

ref: And Jacob told them to be sure to say that he was right behind them. Jacob hoped the gifts would make Esau friendly, so Esau would be glad to see him when they met.
text: na ɛmɔhã behyire ye kyɛ, ‘Asa koso, w’akoaa Yakob li yɛ si.’ ” Osusuli kyɛ, “Ngɔfa akyɛdeɛ bɔ wali me nyunu moa ye ngɔpata ye, na sɛ nziɛ ye me ne ye yɛyia a, mede medi kyɛ okole me kɔto nu.”
translated: And the elder said, ‘Asa is gone, your child Jacob is the one left.’ He continued, ‘Take a gift and give it to my wife so she will accept it, and if she agrees to meet with me, I will come and sit with her.’

ref: Afterwards, Jacob went back and spent the rest of the night alone. A man came and fought with Jacob until just before daybreak.
text: Ɔhale Yakob ngome né, biaa bie ne ye besuale ngɔtɔfaen maa aleɛ hyene.
translated: Jacob is very strong, no one can defeat him in a wrestling match.

ref: The man said, “From now on, your name will no longer be Jacob. You will be called Israel, because you have wrestled with God and with men, and you have won.”
text: Na osele Yakob kyɛ, “Bɛngɔfrɛ wɔ Yakob ko, na mmom Yisrael, ofikyɛ ɛne Nyameɛ ne mmenia apereapereɛ so, na ali ngonim.”
translated: But he said, “Your name will no longer be called Jacob, but rather Israel, because you have struggled with God and with humans and have prevailed.”

ref: Jacob said, “Now tell me your name.” “Don't you know who I am?” he asked. And he blessed Jacob.
text: Yeɛ Yakob sele ye kyɛ, “Mesrɛ wɔ, bɔ wɔ dumaa kyire me.” Na Biaa ne buale kyɛ, “Nzu ati yeɛ ebisa me dumaa ɔ?” Na oyirale Yakob wɔ berɛ.
translated: When Jacob asked his friend, "Please, lend me your knife," Biaa replied, "Is it water that you are asking my knife for?" And Jacob was surprised at that.

ref: As Jacob was on his way back home, some of God's angels came and met him.
text: Yakob sili nu toale ye ateetulɛ ne aso, na Nyameɛ abɔfo mɔ yiale ye.
translated: Jacob lay down to sleep with a stone as his pillow, and God's angels appeared to him.

ref: Jacob sent messengers on ahead to Esau, who lived in the land of Seir, also known as Edom.
text: Yakob tuli ɛbɔfoɛ maa belili ye nyunu bɛhɔle oliɛma Yisɔ berɛ wɔ Sɛre aseɛ so, Edom maen nu,
translated: Jacob saw angels and a ladder stretching from earth to heaven at night in a dream, when he was fleeing from Esau, in the land of Edom.

ref: Jacob was so frightened that he divided his people, sheep, cattle, and camels into two groups.
text: Edwirɛ ne mane ɛserɛ piri hane Yakob; ɔhyɛle mmenia bɔ né bɛboka ye so ne anu nyɔ, ɔne mmoaen ne nandwie ne mbɔngɔ mɔ koso nu kure nyɔ.
translated: Work and sweat are very difficult for Jacob; he wakes up early to take care of his children and wife, and he also goes to the farm with goats, sheep, and dogs to fetch water.

ref: After Jacob had spent the night there, he chose some animals as gifts for Esau:
text: Yiti ɔlale berɛ sɔ kɔngɔen ne, na ɔneaneane ye agyapadeɛ nu penane akyɛdeɛ mane oliɛma Yisɔ:
translated: When you harvest your field, do not go over it again, and do not take the leftover gifts for yourself; leave them for the poor and the stranger.

ref: Then he said to the servant in charge of the first herd, “When Esau meets you, he will ask whose servant you are. He will want to know where you are going and who owns those animals in front of you.
text: Ɔhyɛle bɔ oli anyunu paa ne kyɛ, “Sɛ nniɛma Yisɔ yia wɔ na obisa wɔ kyɛ, ‘Nwa akoaa yeɛ ole wɔ? Ɛkɔ ni? Na ɛhemɔ bɔ beli wɔ nyunu ye nwa deɛ ɔ?’ a,
translated: The teacher always says, "If someone comes to Yisɔ and asks, 'Where is the child? Where did he go? And where does the child who is always with you live?' then,

ref: Afterwards, Jacob went back and spent the rest of the night alone. A man came and fought with Jacob until just before daybreak.
text: Ɔhale Yakob ngome né, biaa bie ne ye besuale ngɔtɔfaen maa aleɛ hyene.
translated: Jacob is very strong, no one can defeat him in wrestling.

ref: Jacob said, “Now tell me your name.” “Don't you know who I am?” he asked. And he blessed Jacob.
text: Yeɛ Yakob sele ye kyɛ, “Mesrɛ wɔ, bɔ wɔ dumaa kyire me.” Na Biaa ne buale kyɛ, “Nzu ati yeɛ ebisa me dumaa ɔ?” Na oyirale Yakob wɔ berɛ.
translated: When Jacob arrived, he said, “Please, give me some water to drink.” But Biaa’s father asked, “Why are you asking me for water?” And Jacob was surprised at that moment.

ref: The sun was coming up as Jacob was leaving Peniel. He was limping because he had been struck on the hip,
text: Mmerɛ bɔ ɔ́pɛ nu wɔ Penuel ne, né eyia afite, na ɔnate ye dwongu bɔ né wafandi ne ati, né ɔtɔ gua so.

ref: But Esau ran toward Jacob and hugged and kissed him. Then the two brothers started crying.
text: Nakoso Yisɔ le amirika ooyiale ye, ɔtɔle ye nu, na ɔfale ye ti tole ye kɔme anwo fele ye noa. Na bɛ munyɔ besuni.
translated: Nakoso Yisɔ is in America studying, he is learning, and he is also working to take care of himself. And he is doing well.

ref: Next, Leah and her children came and bowed down; finally, Joseph and Rachel also came and bowed down.
text: Sɔ ala yeɛ Lea ne ye mma mɔ koso bepingyele berɛ ne, bɛbɔle bɛ nu ase ɔ. Na afei bɔ ɔpɛ ye bo ɔ, Yosef ne Rahɛl koso bepingyele berɛ, na bɛbɔle bɛ nu ase.
translated: When Leah had finished having children, she stopped having them. Then later, when Joseph and Rachel had finished having children, they also stopped having them.

ref: Meanwhile, Jacob heard what had happened. But his sons were out in the fields with the cattle, so he did not do anything at the time.
text: Yakob tele kyɛ Sɛkem ato ɔwa brasua Dina mmɔnaa, nakoso ɔnate kyɛ né ye mma mmienzua nemɔ bafa nandwie bahɔ adidie wɔ eburo ne ati, ɔyɛle konn kɔpem kyɛ besa bɛwale awuro ɔ.
translated: Jacob traveled to Shechem and left his daughter Dinah at home, while he and his two sons went to the field to tend the sheep and eat under the tree, but he returned later to find that a man wanted to marry her.

ref: The two men met with the other leaders of their city and told them:
text: Yiti Hamɔ ne ɔwa Sɛkem bɛhɔle bɛ kuro n’anoa nu dɔ, na besele kuro berɛ mmienzua ne kyɛ,
translated: He left Hamɔ and went to Sɛkem to see how things were in the city, and he stayed in the city for two days and nights.

ref: These people really are friendly. Why not let them move freely about until they find the property they want? There's enough land here for them and for us. Then our families can marry into theirs, and theirs can marry into ours.
text: “Sɔ mmenia ye mɔ, bɛ ne yɛ te asomdwee nu, yiti ɛ́mɔma bɛ́tena aseɛ ye aso na beli bɛ dwaa. Aseɛ ne la berɛ pralalala ma bɛ, yiti ɛ́mɔma yɛ́gyigya bɛ mma mmrasua, na yɛ koso yɛfa yɛ deɛ mɔ yɛma begya.
translated: If the women are well, the home will be peaceful, and the children will stay and listen and not wander. The home is the foundation for everything, and the children learn good things from the women, and we continue to build on what is good for our future.

ref: We have to do only one thing before they will agree to stay here and become one nation with us. Our men will have to be circumcised just like theirs.
text: Nakoso nikyee komapɛ yeɛ sɛ yɛyɔ a mmenia nemɔ kɔpene so na bɛ ne yɛ atena maa yayɛ mmenia ko ɔ: kyɛ yɛ nu biala kɔmaa bɛkɔpɛ ye bienzua kyɛbɔ bapɛ bɛ deɛ ne ɔ.
translated: The hunger I felt was so intense that I had to go out and look for food to satisfy myself: but when I got there, I realized that what I was looking for was not there.

ref: Three days later the men who had been circumcised were still weak from pain. So Simeon and Levi, two of Dinah's brothers, attacked with their swords and killed every man in the town,
text: Ye ɛlɛsa ne aso bɔ bɛ kena ne tutu bɛ nen, Yakob mma nemɔ anu nyɔ bɔ beli Simeon ne Lewi, bɔ bɛte Dina aliɛma mmienzua ne, bɛhwehwene bɛ ngrandɛ. Bɛhɔle kuro ne aso mvum nu, na behuhuni mmienzua ne amukoraati.
translated: When their father heard what had happened to their sister, Jacob’s sons, especially Simeon and Levi, were very angry. They went to the town’s gate, and when they saw their sister’s disgrace, they were furious and plotted revenge.

ref: Jacob's other sons came and took everything they wanted. All this was done because of the horrible thing that had happened to their sister.
text: Yakob mma mmienzua nemɔ fitele awufoɛ nemɔ aso ne, bɛfene kuro ne anu nningyein, ofikyɛ Sɛkem agua b’eliɛma brasua ne anwo fiɛ.
translated: Jacob had two sons who were tending the flock near the graves of their ancestors, close to the town, and one day he sent Joseph to check on his brothers at Shechem and bring back news from home.

ref: While they were there, Rebekah's personal servant Deborah died. They buried her under an oak tree and called it “Weeping Oak.” God Blesses Jacob at Bethel
text: Debora bɔ né ɔte Rebɛka afaneɛ ne wuli, na besiele ye wɔ eluii bie abo wɔ Betel ngwaen; yiti bɛfrɛle berɛ Alɔnbakute.
translated: Deborah, Rebekah’s nurse, died and was buried under the oak below Bethel; so it was named Allon-bacuth.

ref: Jacob set up a tombstone over her grave, and it is still there.
text: Na Yakob fale ngayeɛ nyɔboɛ penane ye asieleɛ ne aso; ɛhe te Rahɛl asieleɛ so ngayeɛ nyɔboɛ bɔ ɔwɔ berɛ ɔbaadwu ɛnɛ ye.
translated: And Jacob lay near the grave of his mother, but he was at Rachel's grave when night fell.

ref: He married three Canaanite women: The first was Adah, the daughter of Elon the Hittite; the second was Oholibamah, the daughter of Anah and the granddaughter of Zibeon the Hivite;
text: Yisɔ gyale ɔye mɔ fili Kanaanfoɛ nu; bɛ ɛhe: Hitiniɛ Elon ɔwa brasua Ada, Ana bɔ ye baba ole Hiwiniɛ Sibeon ne, ɔwa brasua Oholibama;
translated: Esau married women from among the Canaanites; these are: Adah daughter of Elon the Hittite, and Oholibamah daughter of Anah, son of Zibeon the Hivite.

ref: The clans that descended from Esau and Oholibamah the daughter of Anah were Jeush, Jalam, and Korah.
text: Yisɔ aye Oholibama ye mma mmienzua mɔ ɛhe: atitirefoɛ Yeyuse, Yalam ɔne Kora; atitirefoɛ bɔ yeɛ Ana awa brasua Oholibama ɔne Yisɔ bɛwole bɛ hɔ ɛne.
translated: Oholibamah bore these sons to Esau: chiefs Jeush, Jalam, and Korah; these are the chiefs descended from Oholibamah, Esau’s wife, daughter of Anah.

ref: Shobal's sons were Alvan, Manahath, Ebal, Shepho, and Onam.
text: Sobal mma mmienzua mɔ ɛhe: Alwan, Manahate, Ebal, Sefo ne Onam.
translated: These are the names of the five children: Alwan, Manahate, Ebal, Sefo, and Onam.

ref: The clans that descended from Esau took their names from their families and the places where they lived. They are Timna, Alvah, Jetheth,
text: Yisɔ bosofoɛ atitire nemɔ dumaa, kyɛbɔ bɛ mmusua kure, b’atenaleɛ ɔne bɛ dumaa te ɛhe: Atitirefoɛ Timina, Alwa, Yetete,
translated: The chief priest leads the festival, announces to all the families, and the elders who lead the festival are: Chief Timina, Alwa, Yetete.

ref: Jacob loved Joseph more than he did any of his other sons, because Joseph was born when Jacob was very old. Jacob had even given Joseph a fancy coat
text: Né Yisrael kuro Yosef dwirɛ tra ye mma mɔ ngaa ne amu, ofikyɛ ɔte ye nworowaa nu ba. Yiti ɔpale ataadeɛ tendenn bie mane ye.
translated: When Joseph's brothers arrived in Egypt, they did not recognize him, but he recognized them. He was wearing fine linen clothes.

ref: which showed that Joseph was his favorite son, and so Joseph's brothers hated him and would not be friendly to him.
text: Na oliɛma mmienzua nemɔ nwuni kyɛ bɛ baba kuro Yosef dwirɛ tra bɛ mu nen, bɛpɔle ye, yiti né bɛ ne ye dwudwo ɛyaa so.
translated: And two days after the death of the old woman, they went to Joseph’s town to inform him, and when he heard it, he became very sad.

ref: Joseph later had another dream, and he told his brothers, “Listen to what else I dreamed. The sun, the moon, and eleven stars bowed down to me.”
text: Yosef hyene laleɛ bieku, na ɔbɔle oliɛma nemɔ amaneɛ kyɛ, “Ɛ́mɔtie, mahye laleɛ bieku, na nwuni kyɛ eyia, ɛsraen ne nzramaa buru ne ko bɛkoto me.”
translated: Joseph had a dream, and he told his brothers, saying, “Listen, I had a dream, and behold, the sun, the moon, and eleven stars bowed down to me.”

ref: One day when Joseph's brothers had taken the sheep to a pasture near Shechem,
text: Ɛlɛho bie, oliɛma mmienzua nemɔ fale bɛ se mmoaen ne bɛhɔle adidie wɔ Sɛkem.
translated: Some time later, two young women went out to look at the animals and the fields in Shechem.

ref: “Don't murder him or even harm him. Just throw him into a well out here in the desert.” Reuben planned to rescue Joseph later and take him back to his father.
text: Ruben sele bɛ kyɛ, “Nnɛma ɛmɔkã mogya begua, ɛ́mɔgyi ye bɛ́to kumaa ye anu ɛserɛ ye aso wa, na mmom nnɛma ebiala fa ye sa ka ye.” Né wayɛ ye adwene kyɛ ode ye fi bɛ sa nu na ɔfa ye sa kɔma ye baba.
translated: Ruben said to them, "Do not shed blood, throw him into this pit here in the wilderness, but do not lay a hand on him." He said this to rescue him from them and take him back to his father.

ref: Then he went back to his brothers and said, “The boy is gone! What am I going to do?”
text: osa wale oliɛma nemɔ berɛ, na oosele kyɛ, “Abɛɛmaa ne nne berɛ, na nyɔ me nwo sɛ?”
translated: "When the woman was pounding fufu, she stopped and asked, 'Woman, this is your time, so why don't you give birth to me?'"

ref: Joseph's brothers killed a goat and dipped Joseph's fancy coat in its blood.
text: Na bɛfale Yosef ataadeɛ ne, behuni abɔngye, na bɛfale ataadeɛ ne bɛfɔlefɔlele ye mogya ne.
translated: They took Joseph's robe, slaughtered a goat, and dipped the robe in the blood.

ref: After this, they took the coat to their father and said, “We found this! Look at it carefully and see if it belongs to your son.”
text: Bɛfale ataadeɛ tendenn ne bɛbrɛle bɛ baba, na besele kyɛ, “Nea ataadeɛ bie bɔ yanwu ye ɔ, sɛ ɛwa ne deɛ ne ahɔ a.”
translated: The strong cloth seller and the weak one quarreled, and the strong one said, “Whoever’s cloth is exposed to the sun will know if it is truly theirs or not.”

ref: About that time Judah left his brothers in the hill country and went to live near his friend Hirah in the town of Adullam.
text: Na sɔ mmerɛ ne, Yuda fili oliɛma mɔ anwo, na ɔhɔle ɔne Adulamniɛ bie bɔ bɛfrɛ ye Hira bɛɛtenane.
translated: At that time, Judah left his brothers and went down to stay with a man of Adullam named Hirah.

ref: Tamar found out that her father-in-law Judah was going to Timnah to shear his sheep.
text: Na bɛbɔle Tamar amaneɛ kyɛ, “Wɔ sia ɔ́kɔ Timina ɔkɔpepɛ ye mmoaen nwo nwiaa” nen,
translated: And it was told to Tamar, saying, "Behold, your father-in-law is going up to Timnah to shear his sheep."

ref: After returning home, Tamar took off the veil and dressed in her widow's clothes again.
text: Tamar dwɔsole hɔle. Ɔyele ye ngatanyunu ne, na osa fale ye kuna ɛtena ne herale.
translated: Tamar is a beautiful woman. She loves her husband, but he does not treat her well.

ref: Judah asked his friend Hirah take a goat to the woman, so he could get back the ring and walking stick, but she wasn't there.
text: Yuda fale abɔngye akrɔngrɔma ne somane ye damvo Adulamniɛ ne kyɛ ɔfa ɔɔpɔ awowaa nningyein ne ofi braa ne berɛ ɔbrɛ ye. Nakoso ɔhɔle ne, wannwu ye.
translated: Judah left his brothers and went down to stay with a man of Adullam named Hirah. There Judah met the daughter of a Canaanite man named Shua; he married her and made love to her. She became pregnant and gave birth to a son, who was named Er. But Er, Judah’s firstborn, was wicked in the Lord’s sight; so the Lord put him to death.

ref: As Tamar was being dragged off, she sent someone to tell her father-in-law, “The man who gave me this ring, this cord, and this walking stick is the one who got me pregnant.”
text: Na bɛ́fa Tamar befite bɛ́ba ne, ɔsomane maa beesele ye sia ne kyɛ, “Biaa bɔ ole sɔ nningyein hemɔ nen, ye yeɛ me ne ye yɛnwonzɛ ɔ.” Na ɔtoale so sele ye kyɛ, “Mesrɛ wɔ, nea nningyein hemɔ: pɛteaa ye, nyamaa ye, ɔne pɔmaa ye, nwa deɛ ɔ?”
translated: When Tamar's brother came to her, he greeted her and said, "My sister, why are you looking so sad, tell me what is wrong." And she replied, "Please, what is wrong with me: is it hunger, thirst, or is it pain, or what?"

ref: No one in my master's house is more important than I am. The only thing he hasn't given me is you, and that's because you are his wife. I won't sin against God by doing such a terrible thing as this.”
text: Awuro wa de, ebiala nni mu ndra me, yeɛ ɔmva hwee mmɔ me aheɛ, sana wɔ ngome, ofikyɛ ɔye ole wɔ. Nzu ati yeɛ nyɔ sɔ amumuyɛɛ piri ye na nyɔ bɔne ndia Nyameɛ ɔ?”
translated: My husband is here, but there is no food in the house for me; if he doesn’t give me anything, how will I survive, and yet he is proud, thinking he is rich. Is it good for someone to pretend to be good while doing evil things before God?

ref: But when I screamed for help, he left his coat and ran out of the house.”
text: Nakoso nhyɛle ye bo kyɛ meteatea nu ne, ɔyakyili ye ataadeɛ me sa nu, na ɔnwatili fili awuro wa.”
translated: Even if the dress is torn and patched, it is still better than having no clothes at all, and it still covers the body.

ref: and threw Joseph in the same prison where the king's prisoners were kept. While Joseph was in prison,
text: Yiti Yosef ye mene fale Yosef ɔɔtole kuku nu, nekaa bɔ bɛfa Faro kukunufoɛ begua ne, na ɔlale kuku nu.
translated: When Joseph heard that Joseph’s father was sick, he took his two sons to Pharaoh’s palace to see him, and he told his father.

ref: the Lord helped him and was good to him. He even made the jailer like Joseph so much that
text: Nakoso AWURADE gyinane Yosef si, na ɔlole ye; onwuni ye anwumvoin maa kuku nu neafoɛ ne hurole ye dwirɛ.
translated: The Lord was with Joseph, and he prospered; he did not die in the evening but rather his days were filled with joy and his heart was glad.

ref: The jailer did not worry about anything, because the Lord was with Joseph and made him successful in all that he did.
text: Nikyee biala bɔ né owura Yosef sa ne, kuku nu neafoɛ ne anha ye nwo wɔ ye nwo, ofikyɛ né AWURADE gyi Yosef si, na bɔ ɔyɔle biala AWURADE maa ɔyɛle yee.
translated: The woman saw that her master Joseph was handsome, so she desired him, but he did not agree to sleep with her, because the LORD was with Joseph, and everything he did, the LORD made it prosper.

ref: The king's personal servant told Joseph, “In my dream I saw a vine
text: Yiti nzaasulɛfoɛ so panyi ne hane ye laleɛ ne hyirele Yosef kyɛ, “Laleɛ ne anu, né bobe bakaa gyi me nyunu.
translated: The chief of the cupbearers looked at him and said to Joseph, "I had a dream, and here is what happened: there was a vine in front of me."

ref: with three branches. As soon as it budded, it blossomed, and its grapes became ripe.
text: Né bobe ne le asa mmaa nza. Ɔfefɛle pɛ, yeɛ ɔbɔle nyerɛen ɔ, na nyerɛen kure kure ne borole yɛle bobe mmaa.
translated: If a palm nut is not ripe, it does not fall; but when it is fully ripe, it falls by itself.

ref: When the chief cook saw that Joseph had given a good meaning to the dream, he told Joseph, “I also had a dream. In it I was carrying three breadbaskets stacked on top of my head.
text: Na paanootonɛfoɛ so panyi ne tele kyɛ Yosef aha edwirɛ fɛɛ wafa laleɛ ne anwo nen, osele Yosef kyɛ, “Me koso ngyene laleɛ ɔ, né paanoo kɛndɛen nza gyigyi so wɔ me ti so.
translated: And the chief baker also had a dream and told it to Joseph, saying, "I too had a dream, and there were three baskets of bread on my head."

ref: Three days later, while the king was celebrating his birthday with a dinner for his officials, he sent for his personal servant and the chief cook.
text: Ye ɛlɛsa ne aso, né ɔte Faro krakyẽa. Ɔtole poen mane ye mbanyi amukoraa. Na ɔyele nzaasulɛfoɛ so panyi ne paanootonɛfoɛ so panyi ne fili kuku nu maa beegyinane ye mbanyi nemɔ anyunu.
translated: He was a wise man, and he lived in ancient Egypt. He was known by many people. And he was respected by both the elders and the young, and his good name was known everywhere among the people.

ref: Two years later the king of Egypt dreamed he was standing beside the Nile River.
text: Afoɛ nyɔ mumua si, Faro hyene laleɛ kyɛ ogyi asue Naele noa.
translated: When there was a famine, Pharaoh saw a dream and called Joseph to interpret it.

ref: Later, seven other heads of grain appeared, but they were thin and scorched by a wind from the desert.
text: Mvum nu, onwuni kyɛ aburoo nzoo bɔ bangye na eyia afiteleɛ mvrama akese bɛ ɔ afifi.
translated: In the olden days, the dead were buried with maize so that their spirit would have enough food for the journey.

ref: The thin heads of grain swallowed the seven full heads. Again the king woke up, and it had only been a dream.
text: Na aburoo kesenewaa nemɔ memene aburoo nzoo bɔ bahyẽ nemɔ. Afei Faro nye tele, na onwuni kyɛ laleɛ yeɛ wahye ɔ.
translated: And the big corn was with me, I was planting corn on the farm. Then Pharaoh sent for me, and I stayed there until evening before returning.

ref: When you were angry with me and your chief cook, you threw us both in jail in the house of the captain of the guard.
text: Nana, mmerɛ bɔ ɛfɛle wɔ ngoaa so yaa, na ɛhyele me ne paanootonɛfoɛ so panyi ne guale kuku nu wɔ angɔbeahene awuro ne,
translated: Nana, when the time was exactly right on the farm, it affected me and the elders as well as the chief drummer while drinking palm wine at the palm wine chief's house.

ref: The king sent for Joseph, who was quickly brought out of jail. He shaved, changed his clothes, and went to the king.
text: Yiti Faro somane maa bɛɛfrɛle Yosef bɛmane ye. Bɛyele ye befili kumaa nu kuku nu berɛ ndɛndɛ. Na Yosef poane ye kanza na ɔsesane ye ndaadeɛ yiele ne, ɔwale oogyinane Faro nyunu.
translated: Afterwards, Pharaoh called Joseph and spoke to him. He brought him out of the prison quickly. Then Joseph shaved himself, changed his clothes, and went to stand before Pharaoh.

ref: “Your Majesty,” Joseph answered, “I can't do it myself, but God can give a good meaning to your dreams.”
text: Yosef buale Faro kyɛ, “Nana, me de mengora ye bo kyire, na mmom Nyameɛ yeɛ ɔkɔma Faro nyeanoɛ paa ɔ.”
translated: Joseph answered Pharaoh, “Sir, I cannot interpret dreams, but God will give Pharaoh the answer he desires.”

ref: I saw seven fat, healthy cows come up out of the river, and they began feeding on the grass.
text: Na nwuni kyɛ nandwie mmeleɛ branoa nzoo bɔ bɛ nwo yɛ nyemene fili asue Naele nu beedidili ngyɛngyɛma ne anu.
translated: When someone dies, on the seventh day the family gathers to mourn and perform rituals so that the deceased's spirit may rest in peace.

ref: I also dreamed that I saw seven heads of grain growing on one stalk. The heads were full and ripe.
text: Nza ngyene laleɛ nwuni aburoo nzoo bɔ bahyẽ paa, na bɛwɔ bakaa ko aso ɔ.
translated: If you plant maize during the dry season, it will not grow well, and you will not get a good harvest.
